sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! The charcoal moved to cols 6-9, and the light green strip moved from cols 8-9 to cols 4-5! The green strip followed the charcoal. So the green strip is attached to the charcoal, like the yellow tail in Level 1.
So the green strip is the "tail" of the charcoal, similar to the yellow tail in Level 1. When the charcoal moves, the tail moves with it.
In Level 1, the goal was to move the charcoal to the leftmost position. The charcoal had a yellow tail attached to its right side. In Level 2, the charcoal has a green tail attached to its left side.
So the mechanics are similar, but the tail is on the opposite side. In Level 1, the tail was on the right, and the charcoal moved left to complete the level. In Level 2, the tail is on the left, so maybe the goal is to move the charcoal to the rightmost position?
Let me try moving the charcoal all the way right to see if the level completes.
The charcoal is currently at cols 6-9. The rightmost position is cols 22-25. I need to move it right by (22-6)/4 = 4 clicks.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: The light green strip is attached to the charcoal as a tail (like the yellow tail in Level 1). When charcoal moves, the tail moves with it. In Level 1, tail was on right, goal was move left. In Level 2, tail is on left, so goal might be to move RIGHT to the rightmost position.
Plan: Move charcoal all the way right (4 clicks from cols 6-9 to cols 22-25).

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Move charcoal all the way right
print("Moving charcoal all the way right...")
for i in range(4):
    action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 46, 'col': 2}])
    seg = current_frame.segmentation
    for n in seg['nodes']:
        if n['color'] == 'c' and n['pixels'] == 16:
            cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
            print(f"  After click {i+1}: charcoal cols = {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
    if last_action_result.get('level_completed') or last_action_result.get('done'):
        print("  Level completed!")
        break

print(f"\nLevel completed? {last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")
print(f"Done? {last_action_result.get('done', False)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
